spa: Spa? I won here the year I took the title — hauled it through Eau Rouge flat with Mika Häkkinen's mirrors full of me. You never forget a lap like that.
I love this place. Schumacher and I went side by side up through Raidillon once, neither lifting. Bravest thing I ever did in a car.
Senna in the rain here — I watched it from twelve seconds behind, which felt like front-row seats to history.
Prost told me the secret of Spa over dinner once: 'slow hands, fast car.' Took me five years to understand it.
monza: Monza! I clinched a championship here, slipstreaming Schumacher to the line into the Parabolica. The tifosi nearly brought the grandstands down.
I out-dragged Barrichello to the flag through these Lesmos once — by a car length. The Temple of Speed gave me one of my greatest days.
I had Berger and Alesi either side of me into the first chicane here — three cars, two spaces. The tifosi still owe me for braking last.
Mansell gave me a tow down the straight here in my rookie year, then chopped me at the Parabolica. Lesson delivered, free of charge.
silverstone: Silverstone's my back garden — I won my home Grand Prix here, wheel to wheel with Rubens through Becketts. Best day of my career, bar none.
Maggotts and Becketts at full chat against Häkkinen — that's as alive as I ever felt in a racing car.
I won my home... well, MY Silverstone here against Damon Hill — through Copse flat, both of us, neither blinking. British crowds make you brave.
Häkkinen and I swapped fastest laps here all afternoon once. Maple-smooth, that man. The crowd got their money's worth.
suzuka: Suzuka decided my title one year — me and Schumacher nose to tail through the esses, neither of us breathing. Ice in the veins, that's what it took.
130R in my day was properly frightening, flat out with Mika alongside. This place sorts the brave from the rest, always has.
A title went through 130R in my day — mine, as it happens. Flat or lift? I didn't lift. Don't tell my mother.
Schumacher and I had our best and worst races here. Suzuka gives you nothing — that's exactly why drivers love it.
imola: Imola — we raced hard here in my F1 days, wheel to wheel with Häkkinen into Tamburello. A circuit that demanded total respect, and still does.
I won here once managing the car to the flag while Schumacher chewed his tyres up behind. Patience took that one.
Alesi around Tosa with the Ferrari crowd roaring — you could feel it through the chassis. Imola has a heartbeat, this place.
Prost schooled me at Piratella in testing here — one corner, three lines, and his was always the fastest. Homework, homework, homework.
interlagos: Interlagos! I came from nowhere to win here in the wet — Barrichello chasing me the whole way, the crowd roaring for him. Pure magic.
Anti-clockwise, bumpy, hard on the neck — I took a title down to the wire here against Mika. Loved every second.
Racing Senna in front of his own crowd here — you weren't racing a man, you were racing a nation. Unforgettable.
A very young Barrichello followed me round here in testing once. By lunch he was following me no longer. Brazil breeds them quick.
hungaroring: Budapest — I won here from the second row, couldn't pass Schumacher for thirty laps until he finally blinked. Patience won me that one.
Monaco without the walls, this — I learned to bide my time here, then pounce. Beat Häkkinen to the flag doing exactly that.
I lost a race here to Damon Hill by driving faster and thinking slower. The Hungaroring is a chess board with kerbs.
Piquet used to say you win here on Thursday, in the setup sheets. He was right — overtaking is for optimists at this place.
hockenheim: Hockenheim, the old long blast through the forest — I out-dragged Häkkinen to the line here for a famous win. Proper old-school racing.
Into that stadium section with Schumacher alongside and ninety thousand Germans screaming for him — you don't forget that.
The OLD Hockenheim — Berger and I flat through the forest, engines screaming, hearts in mouths. Different sport back then.
The stadium section with forty thousand Germans on their feet — Schumacher's crowd. Racing him here was like an away final.
nurburg: The Nürburgring — I sealed a title here once, holding off Schumacher when the rain came down. Kept my head while others lost theirs.
This GP loop is fiddlier than it looks. I beat Rubens here by reading the weather better than he did.
Alesi in the rain here was frightening from BEHIND — I can't imagine the view from his seat. The Eifel weather makes heroes hourly.
Frentzen's home patch — he knew every camber change. Local knowledge is worth two tenths here, and he collected every one.
barcelona: Barcelona — endless testing in my F1 days, then I won the race too, nursing the tyres while Rubens cooked his. Knowledge is everything here.
That final sector cost Häkkinen a win against me once — he ran wide, I pounced. This place punishes the smallest greed.
A thousand testing laps here with Coulthard — we knew every bump by its first name. Race day still surprised us. Barcelona always does.
Mansell and Senna side by side down the straight in ninety-one — I was watching from the garage thinking, that's the standard, then.
zandvoort: Zandvoort — those banked corners were a thrill even in my F1 car. I won here side by side with Mika through Tarzan. Wonderful place.
I went round the OUTSIDE of Eddie Irvine at Tarzan once. He still disputes it. The banking remembers, Eddie.
Sea air, sand on the kerbs, and a crowd on the dunes — Zandvoort felt like racing at the beach. Still does.
red bull ring: The old Österreichring up here was lightning fast — I took a win battling Schumacher up and down this hillside. Brakes glowing every lap.
Berger's home hills. He drove here like the mountains were cheering him on — and honestly, they were.
Short lap, no hiding. Villeneuve told me here: 'every mistake shows twice a minute.' Then he made none. Annoying man.
macau: Macau! I did the Grand Prix here as a junior — hit the wall at Fisherman's twice in one weekend and still call it the best race of my life.
Every young hotshot of my generation came here to prove themselves. The barriers at Macau took more careers than the stopwatch ever did.
Macau in F3 — Coulthard, Schumacher, me, and barriers everywhere. The bravest field I ever raced in, before any of us were famous.
You don't win Macau. You survive it faster than the others. The Guia circuit taught me more than a season of testing.
brands hatch: Brands Hatch is where I won my first ever single-seater race — Paddock Hill Bend in the wet, heart in my mouth all the way down.
My dad watched me from the bank at Druids every single weekend as a kid. This place isn't a circuit to me, it's home.
British F3 at Brands — Paddock Hill Bend sorted the pretenders by lap two. I still get butterflies at the crest.
I raced Damon Hill here before either of us had a proper salary. The racing was free; the lessons were priceless.
paul ricard: I did thousands of testing miles at Ricard in my Formula One days. I know every centimetre of that blue run-off — mostly from spinning across it.
We tested here for weeks on end. Häkkinen and I once raced hire cars around the paddock roads at midnight — I'm saying nothing more.
Winter testing on the Mistral with Prost setting the benchmark — you'd chase his time all week and he'd find another second on Friday.
All that run-off now! In my day the Mistral straight was flat, blind faith at three hundred. Ask Alain about the old chicane.
zolder: Zolder's old-school Belgium — trees, kerbs and consequences. I raced touring cars here after my rally days. Loved every terrifying minute.
The chicanes at Zolder eat brave men. I've got the bent suspension bills to prove it.
Zolder is old-school Belgium — trees, kerbs, consequences. I cut my teeth in junior formula here and left some bodywork as payment.
The chicanes here punish greed. I learned that three times in one weekend once. Slow in, fast out, stay humble.
watkins: Watkins Glen — the old home of the US Grand Prix. The history in these kerbs, you can feel it through the steering wheel.
The Glen in the autumn, leaves turning, engines echoing off the hills — if you don't love this, you don't love racing.
Watkins Glen — I guested in America once and Mario Andretti showed me the line through the Esses. You don't forget a tour guide like that.
The Glen's got history in its guardrails. American fans knew every lap I'd ever driven — humbling, that.
anderstorp: Anderstorp! The Swedish Grand Prix ran here on a runway in a bog — flattest track in the world and one of the trickiest to read.
They used the airstrip as the back straight here. Half aerodrome, half racetrack, all Sweden. Wonderful place.
Sweden! When I went rallying, Grönholm told me Anderstorp was the only circuit Swedes forgive for not being a forest.
A runway straight and airfield corners — Anderstorp is quirky, and quirky tracks reward drivers who stay curious.
norisring: The Norisring is Germany's only proper street race — two hairpins, a flat-out blast, and zero forgiveness. I love it.
It's braking, braking and more braking here. By half distance your right leg belongs to someone else.
I did a DTM guest drive here against Bernd Schneider — street-fighting in touring cars. Hardest 'holiday' I ever took.
Two hairpins and a wall — the Norisring is a boxing ring with a grandstand. The crowd is close enough to hear your downshifts.
sachsenring: The Sachsenring flows like a rally stage — blind crests, camber everywhere. Honestly one of my favourite laps in this game of ours.
The bike boys own this place, but it's a mighty lap in anything. That downhill run to the final corner is proper bravery.
Rally Germany taught me these Saxon roads — Loeb made tarmac look like a video game. The Sachsenring has that same flowing menace.
The Omega corner here drops away like a rally crest — commit blind, trust the notes. Old habits serve me well watching this place.
